key_insight: In vivo glioma tumors with lower fluidity and viscosity appear to reflect vimentin-linked extracellular matrix remodeling and collective cell migration.
clinical_relevance: MRI-based mechanical imaging could provide a noninvasive biomarker of glioma invasiveness and tumor biology beyond standard anatomy-focused scans.
method: MRE-based cerebral tomoelastography measured in vivo tumor fluidity and viscosity.
Automated histology quantified vimentin-associated extracellular matrix remodeling.
Nuclear aspect ratio measurements captured cellular elongation, with support from actin-vimentin gel experiments.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports correlated patient imaging, histological quantification, and mechanistic tissue-mimicking gel experiments linking the viscoelastic signature to migration-related features.
watch_item: These findings are correlational and need larger prospective validation before viscosity can be used as a routine clinical biomarker.
paper_title: In-vivo glioma viscosity and fluidity as clinical tumor markers of vimentin expression and collective cell migration
